summary: The fundamental counting principle multiplies the number of choices at each step; permutations count ordered arrangements, using factorials.

formulas.name: Permutations of n items taken r at a time

explanation: The fundamental counting principle says that if one choice can be made in $n_1$ ways, a second in $n_2$ ways, and so on, the total number of ways to make all the choices in sequence is the product $n_1 \times n_2 \times \cdots$. A permutation is an arrangement where order matters (e.g. who finishes 1st, 2nd, 3rd). The number of ways to arrange all $n$ distinct items in order is $n!$ (n factorial). The number of ways to arrange only $r$ of $n$ items, in order, is $P(n,r) = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!}$ — equivalent to applying the counting principle directly (n choices for the first slot, $n-1$ for the second, etc.).
